children.text:
Alright. Rasheedah, in the past, especially in the context of your work,
you’ve spoken about the importance of Octavia Butler, ugh, shaping your
vision of Black futures. Could you talk a little about what that means
in particular around questions of memory, community, and action, and how
that has sort of shaped your approach to your work?

annotation.text:
Yeah, absolutely. So I guess I’ll start by naming that Octavia
Butler’s work

in particular was really transformative for me.
Going back to childhood, I’ve loved science fiction, but by the time I
was in college, I had really distanced myself from it because I rarely
saw reflections of myself in terms of blackness, queerness, anything
that reflected my own personal identities. And in college, I had been
given frameworks and language to start to interrogate what those
absences were. So, I was taking classes, like Black Philosophy and Black
Womanhood that was just like, okay, my eyes are open to, like, the
absence of myself in these worlds that I’ve always enjoyed. And then I
had a friend give me a copy of

and it was just really like a
revelation, as most people who feel when they encounter her -Octavia
Butler’s - work for the first time. So this was a time travel story
that was really unlike any of the ones that I grew up on.

children.text:
One that centered a young black woman whose body itself became that
conduit for time travel and where survival and memory persevere, not
just because of her lineage, but because of the possibilities of
community across time. And so rather than, like, focusing on paradoxes
or machines - which are also really fun and I really enjoy that kind of
time travel as well, but - her narrative was really about how history
lives in the body, how it lives in family, how it lives in
relationships, and about the kind of actions that are required to
preserve those connections, umm, particularly in the face of systemic
erasure. So that was really foundational for me, and it really showed me
the ways that memory and community are not just, like, subjects of
speculative fiction, but they’re really truly technologies for
surviving the sort of timelines that we find ourselves on as Black
people. And so that really shaped my own trajectory.

It gave me the confidence that I needed to found the Afrofuturist
Affair, which was my first community-based experiment in practicing time
travel through art and performance and being in community and archiving.
And then later co-founding Black Quantum Futurism with my partner, Camae
and we just literally translated Octavia Butler’s lessons and the
non-linear temporalities that one finds in her work, into sound and
space and community tools. One of our first projects as Black Quantum
Futurism was a sound project called "Constellation Eight," which sampled
her novels into these, like, layered audio collages. And that was really
a direct attempt to sonically render some of the ways that she treats
time as recursive and multiple. And so that became a blueprint for how
we work together today in the present Black Quantum Futurism. The album
came out over 10 years ago. That’s just an example of how we try to
treat memory as material and community as a form of archive.
Yeah, a lot of that is inspired by her work.

I really appreciate that answer. And I’ve always looked to your work in
the way that you kind of make Octavia Butler’s writing a kind of
blueprint for a lot of action. And other people over the years have
joined in that conversation. I think for the readers of Scholarly
Editing, which is, you know, a publication for the Association for
Documentary Editing, they’d be really intrigued about the way that your
work has always shown a concern about how we might imagine the archive
as a space of existence. And I wonder what you would say, what do people
need to do inside and outside of our institutions to understand ways
they could create archives or recreate archives as a way to resist
erasure? Like, what does that mean from your perspective?

It’s a nuanced answer, but I think, ultimately, it’s about treating
the archive very differently than we have. In Western culture, we think
about the archive as something that is, you know, if it’s documenting
history, it’s definitely fixed in time and it has these sort of fixed
definitions and it operates on linear timelines. And I think those kinds
of archives are, again, they’re usually built on a historical record,
and we as Black people have been positioned outside of that record,
right? So it gets really complicated. We’re positioned as people who
don’t belong - either we don’t belong or we’re seriously behind on
the timeline of progress. For us to archive can’t just be about
preserving the past. That past isn’t necessarily meant for us. And so I
think about the archive as rememory, as something that can disrupt time,
about something that create portals to the sort of futures that we’ve
been denied.

So I think about the archive, not just as historical record, but as
future record, as it can hold future record, it can hold these sort of
nonlinear relationships to time. And I think there’s ways to trouble
it, to question what counts as evidence, to sort of create our own
records. I think speculation plays a huge role in that, particularly for
people who have been cut off from time, cut off from our histories,
particularly due to enslavement. Speculation plays a huge role in how I
think about archiving or think about reconnecting back to family
histories that I don’t, you know, have sort of everyday access to or
material access to. And then that can also allow the archive to shift
from being something that’s housed only in institutions like
universities and museums to something that can be more organic and
community-based and dynamic. And that’s how we have tended to think
about archives.

Like, typically, when you’re thinking about archives in a Western
sense, it’s this notion of trust and paternalism that goes into it.
Like, who is the authority to hold the archive, who can hold it you
know, carefully without putting a fingerprint on it and all these sorts
of things? But how can we house our histories and our futures in our own
communities, in the community organization that’s down the street that
you know, maybe there’s a leak in their ceiling, but, right, they’re
going to hold onto those things as long as they can? And so also housing
these things that aren’t, in my practice, maybe aren’t legible to
certain people, you know, Jet magazines, those kinds of things, those
old things that - maybe there’s a collection in the Schaumburg, but a
lot of people aren’t necessarily archiving or holding onto those things
because, again, they’ve been told that there’s only a particular way
that you can archive or you can hold that record.

So yeah, so I think basically to trouble these things and what
Afrofuturism and Black futurism offers us is just that sole possibility
of someone other than the institution being that carrier of our
archives, that carrier of our histories and our memories. And I think an
Afrofuturist approach demands that they open up and that they recognize
that nonlinear relationship and possibility. In our work, we have a
project called "Project Time Capsule" that’s thinking about how we in
the present reach back to add to the historical record and add and be in
touch directly with the past and with our ancestors and give them
information that they can then use to survive or create technologies. So
just rethinking that linear relationship or even that physical tangible
one that has to be legible in this particular way.

I think for a lot of people, they will think of Afrofuturism as a
literary movement or they’ll think of, like, Black Panther as a sort of
an aesthetic, but a lot of your work, an essential way, so it talks
about Afrofuturism, it’s like a lived practice that people can do every
day. And I wonder, especially in the context of what you just said about
thinking differently about what an archive can be. And again, I think
about this in the context of the readership of this journal who are
people who work with very traditional - some of them - are people who
work very traditional kinds of things, papers. They work with the
recollection of institutions. They are working within a system where
whatever comes in has to go through a process to be made at some level,
for lack of a better term, legible. And so there’s, I think, an
inherent challenge to what you just said, because you’re saying to the
people who sort of run the archive, 'you have to think about a totally
different set of things as archives,' which is not to say they have not
done that in the past.

Obviously, given our technological evolution over the last 50 years,
things that were not in the archive 60 years ago are a really important
part of the archive and a whole set of conversations about preservation,
stabilization, and pushing on things forward. But it’s also true,
perhaps less clearly, that on the community side, there’s a
conversation that has to be had about how are we approaching the
archive? Like, what are we doing to remember? I wonder if you could talk
a little bit about what does the community, from your perspective, the
people outside the institution, what do they need to do to remember?

Yeah, that’s such a good question. And I think the answer shifts
depending on the community and depending on what the community needs and
wants to remember and how that remembrance contributes to that community
being able to continue and being able to move forward in whatever sense
that community needs to. I think about all of the communities that have
been harmed by a certain kind of violence and how, according to the
official archive\-- like, let’s just take Tulsa, for example, how the
people in that community did not know what had happened to their
ancestors for many decades because of the level of silence that was
around it, right? Not only just the level of silence within official
archives and the official story, but the pain and the trauma that needed
to be unlocked in a very careful, particular way that, you know, it
couldn’t be passed down even in a way that we think of in oral
traditions.

It had to be unlocked at a certain time when people were able to hold
the level of trauma, and then the action that needed to happen to gain
restitution or reparation for some of the things that had happened. And
so, I think it really depends on the community, and it really depends on
what is the infrastructure, what’s the level of care that can be put
into that. And that is not a question that an outsider can or should
answer, in my opinion, right. And so yeah, this may be challenging to
say, but I think opening up who we consider to be an archivist and who
gets that official designation is part of the answer to that. And again,
that communities should have the level of autonomy to care for their
archives in a way that they think is best, even if that means
forgetting, even if that means we’re not ready to encounter these
memories, like, that should be okay.

These things shouldn’t be forced on people, in my opinion. So yeah,
that’s kind of how I think about it. And also, it’s back to this
question of legibility. I think we can also, even as people who think
expansively about this, be indoctrinated into thinking that things have
to be tangible, be visible in this particular way. And I think archives
can contribute to that, right? Like that there has to be a thing there
that we can all see and understand, but that archiving and passing down
of memory can also be this thing that is totally nonphysical, totally
about language, about words, about certain kinds of sound that trigger
ancestral memory that you can connect it back to that. And it’s like,
how do you, without a mediating technology, hold a sound or archive a
sound, archive particular words, particular way that you say it and a
particular context that you say it in.

Just like, writing that down doesn’t do the thing. That’s for me what
the living piece of it is, is that ... And that defies time. Again,
when we archive something and we put it into a box or put it into a
physical medium that can be revived, we’re in some ways flattening it
as a living thing. We’re concretizing it in some way. And I think that
there are ways to let things live that culture today doesn’t quite
align with that, like the rush to document everything, to sort of like
prove to each other that a thing happened. I understand it, but I think
if we’re truly talking about challenging what an archive is and what
its possibility is, we have to be thinking about the intangible as a
form of archiving.

Yeah. I think that makes a lot of sense given the nature of a lot of
your work. And I think for our readers, it will be interesting to sort
of hear you reflect on a consistent sort of strand in your thinking and
work [that] has talked about the master’s map and the master’s
clock, like time and space, and underscored at some level the kind of
repetitive damage - these are my words, not necessarily your words -
associated with the institutional record, which is a really complicated
set of conversations that I have with students because, of course, the
record itself doesn’t like nod like in a movie, 'I’m the bad guy,' it
just sort of exists as a record. And you have to, especially from the
perspective of communities that are affected by it, articulate the
nature of the injury in a very particular way. At the same time, I think
one of the things that also undergirds your work is a belief at some
very basic level that better structures can be made, not just simply
that this structure sucks, but there are also better structures that can
be made.

And so how you navigate that constant struggle to make clear the nature
of these sort of epistemologies that are grounded in a very particular
kind of remembering, erasure, misremembering, displacement, lots of
words I could rattle off, but at the same time say, 'hey, we can make
other systems' and also recognize, which I think you often do, the
current infrastructure that our system rests on has to be transformed.
Like, that’s a big arc that involves a lot of institutional,
individual, governmental, third dimension, who knows kind of
interventions. And I wonder, how do you navigate that from a practical
standard? Like, that’s an everyday set of questions that doesn’t seem
to have an easy answer.

Yeah, I would definitely agree. I think those things that you named
don’t lend themselves to a neat sequence or order of things or
linearity, if you will, in terms of how you approach them and that they
require community. I mean, that is why I am so much about community. And
I don’t romanticize community as like, 'oh, there’s this perfect set
of people out there who, you know, can activate the will of the people.
I don’t think of it that way. I think there’s a lot of tension, a lot
of struggle that comes with community. Being a part of community,
choosing to be in it with community comes with a lot, but it is
necessary to do all those things, to transform all those things because
there are so many different parts of it. One of the places where I think
it does start is just by naming these things, naming what these things
are, naming the master’s tools for what they are, unsettling the basic
level of objectivity that we have when we look, or that we perceive when
we look at a clock or a map. Understanding these things to be
instruments of control, understanding the ways in which these things,
that the everyday things, the watch that’s on our wrist, the clock
that’s on our wall, how they are tied to the bigger things around
colonial timelines, colonial geographies, being able to talk about and
do a deep dive research on the Prime Meridian Conference where the
standard time zones around the globe were set, right?

That certainly, if you are thinking about it and making those
connections can upset your notion of the clock as a neutral item just
measuring time. So, I think for me, that’s where a lot of it starts, is
just naming that truth behind it, that simple truth that can be buried
by the level of objectivity that we are forced into to be a part of this
society that we’re in. You know, when I think about dismantling, a
piece of that is refusing that authority. So being able to not just say
the record’s wrong, but utilizing the tools, even if it is the
master’s tools that we have to flip and subvert, but using it to
challenge the record, using it to question standardized time, using it
to trouble institutional archives and add into the story. And again,
even if it is speculation, there’s so much value in that.

And then, you know, then when it comes to building the structures and
transforming the structures, I don’t believe everything has to be made
anew because none of these things are new. And actually, these things
have been manipulated. A thing that could be served as a positive tool,
like a clock, have been manipulated in bad ways, so I don’t think we
need to rebuild a whole new \... I think clock time has a utility, but
it has to be agreed upon by a community. It can’t be a globalized thing
coming down on high that, you know, flattens all of us into one
timeline. I don’t think every structure needs to be rebuilt anew. I
think we can take what is already existing, subvert them, reclaim them,
reuse them in new ways and different ways that honor our own
temporalities and spatialities and in ways that we want to move forward
within our communities, whatever we call community.

You know, that’s a really powerful answer, especially in light of the
moment that we’re in related to technology and innovation. And this is
where both our readership at the journal and the people who follow you
will be really interested in your take on AI and the sort of overarching
narrative related to that. I think there are lots of different questions
that might be asked of someone like you, and then particularly you,
because you are so sensitive to the nature of, like, these sort of
built-in structure that place or erase or manipulate. But from your
perspective, how do you see regenerative AI intersecting with, or
possibly disrupting, the kinds of practices that are already
established? Right? Like, is there a way to leverage these tools for
something good to sort of create a kind of Black time practice that
makes sense to you. But also, you know, given the nature of the rapid
transformation that we’ve already seen in a very short period of time,
what is your fear for the way that the community vision or community
archive, the sort of stories that we tell about ourselves, might be
distorted in AI in new and perhaps more sinister ways?

And so, I’m curious, is there a positive path forward represented by
the instability that we’re in, from your perspective, if we can act? On
the other hand, what are the things that we need to be concerned with
given what we know?

Yeah, great question. I mean, I think you’ve already named what I feel
around, ... I have a sense of double consciousness around AI. On the
one hand, it’s deeply problematic for some of the reasons that you
name. It’s built on extraction. It is theft of work, all types of
copyright violations, drains community resources, literally, in terms
of, like, water and energy. We’re seeing it actively pollute
communities and in doing so, it’s reproducing colonial logics, right?
It’s reproducing that logic of theft, taking without consent. And for
Black time practices and community archives that are already fighting
erasure, this extraction is just another very dangerous form of
dispossession. Certainly "sinister," I think is the right word. But on
the other hand, right, I can’t ignore its potential. I think in a way,
AI replicates that collective intelligence. It, you know, It’s similar
in a way that the internet does, but it takes it a step further.

Like it can gather these fragments across time and scale and sort of
mold it into a kind of collective unconscious, even if it is sort of
artificial in a sense. And that is troubling when it’s done
unethically, but I think it also reveals other ways of thinking about
knowledge outside of Western copyright and ownership. And I like that
potential. Even as a person who’s like written a book and sometimes I
feel some type of way about citations and copyright, if I’m thinking
about the logics of ownership and copyright outside of the Western
tradition, I think it actually more mimics what AI is doing and what is
possible for collective knowledge, but only if we can find an ethical
way to do that. And I think it also, like, offers a repository of
imagination that if we steward it differently, it could align with some
of our Black traditions of shared memory and remix and oral circulation.

I see the potential, I see, you know, a way to harness it, but I don’t
know if we’re too far down the road to be able to reclaim it. I like to
think not, but I think it’s done so much harm and how the public
perceives it, we may be too far down the road to really be able to
utilize the power and the potential of it, so...

I’m curious about one of the things you mentioned there, because I
think one of the things that, especially for the readership of Scholarly
Editing, is that we’re actively trying to bring into focus a more
global sense of the archive and the documentary record. And you
mentioned there’s a way that AI might better mimic patterns that we see
in non-Western context. And can you talk a little bit more about if you
were to imagine an AI kinda doing what you wanted to do on a large
scale, what would that look like and how would that change the sort of
practices that people understand? Because I think one of the things
about this particular moment, you’re not the only one who thinks, 'has
this horse left the barn, so to speak? Are we too far down that rabbit
hole?' And I think part of the reason we think that is because we never
get to hear anything else.

It’s so hard to separate our fear and other people longing from the
technology that I’d love for you to sort of talk and imagine like,
'hey, this is what I would like to see AI do and why that’s important'
because I think that’s an important jumping off point for a potential
intervention in practice, right, if we can understand what you’re
meaning by that.

Mmm, this is an interesting question. I haven’t quite ever tried to
imagine it. I think part of what I appreciate about it is that, and why
I kind of see it as mimicking sort of a collective unconsciousness is
the speed at which it can return information and not just, like, return
information, but crafted into something that is coherent and responsive
and mimics thinking. It’s too deep to go into, like, 'is it thinking or
not?' I don’t think it’s thinking in the way that we think, but it’s
doing something that resembles that and it’s doing it at the speed in
which we perceive it very quickly. And I think if we could like, mimic
that, but have a way that it’s connected more deeply to being able to
trace and not just hallucinate the source of things, because I think
that’s part of the issue too, is that, like, it makes up stuff.

Again, beholden to Western citation practices, there has to be a happy
medium. When I think about that, I think about how we do it in law
really is, it is encouraged that you copy, like, no citation, right,
like, because you couldn’t get anything done if everything that you
were doing as a lawyer was original in some way. So you were perfectly
encouraged to copy templates. I was using templates from the 1960s that
somebody used and to, like, perfect them, but you don’t need to credit
this particular person. It’s understood that that information is being
passed down and that you build on that information. And even when it
comes to writing memos or writing papers, you’re not like, 'this was
authored by such and such,' right. Like, the lawyer whose name goes on
it may not be the lawyer who wrote it. So, just that sharing of
information. And I think also very parallel in the scientific community.

You have your rockstar scientists who stick out, but for the most part,
like, folks aren’t in it to get credit. They’re like building out a
body of knowledge that can be tapped into for you know, years to come,
forever, and it continues to build. That’s why you only know a handful
of scientists’ names because that’s not, you know, that's not really
the trajectory. These things already exist in the world. We don’t have
to necessarily, again, build anything new, but we can perfect AI to be
more ethical and to incorporate these sorts of things that can actually
do the work. And I’ve seen memes about it like, 'I've wanted AI to help
me wash dishes so that I could do more thinking,' right. Like, If
there’s more utility to it, which I think it actually does have a lot
of utility, but a lot of it is just based on public perception and
narrative in terms of what’s going to be possible for it because if
people don’t want to improve on it because it’s seen as unethical and
you’re seen as a pariah if you’re using it, I don’t know, that
potential could be squandered.

Well, you mentioned your legal training, and I think for the readers,
they will garner from your introduction to this interview that you are a
lawyer and you’re a housing advocate and you have a long career and an
organization called PolicyLink that is dedicated to these questions. And
I’ve noted over the years looking at your work that one of the things
that ironically has been very powerful is imagination. It’s a kind of
critical imagination that intervenes in a particular set of practices,
and also being able to translate the kind of lived experience of the
people you’re advocating for into a set of, at some level, actions that
they can take to transform their situation. You know, as we sort of look
at our current set of circumstances where perhaps two fundamental things
are under attack: one, the rule of law and what we mean when we say
that, and the validity of the archive, meaning what was supposedly safe
is no longer, especially for a readership that are in many ways, I would
argue, directly connected to both that sort of legal, like, who are the
papers that, the papers of judges, the papers of government, the papers
of this and that, and what institutions and legal cases, and very strong
kind of archival identities associated with these things.

What would you tell these sort of traditional institutional actors as a
person who’s been operating at some level as their own nonprofit, very
rooted in a grassroots effort to empower people? I would make an
argument at some level, almost everyone’s got to learn from someone
like you now if they want to, sort of, like, be able to find an impact.
And I don’t mean that to be joking. I mean, if you think about the
nature of structures and how much they have decayed, what can they learn
from the work of an organization like PolicyLink? What can they learn
from a grassroots organization dedicated to the work that you have done
so many years?

Your observations around our archive and law... we’re seeing these
things being dismantled severely and quickly. And I think it tells us a
lot. And I think part of it is the danger of what I talked about
earlier, which is that archives being in control of state-backed
institutions and us relying on them to hold our memory. So, whether
it’s the Library of Congress or your local museum, the level of
authority and power that they have taken to be able to hold our things,
this was always the natural result of that. This was always going to be
the outcome of digitally archiving Black oral histories and photos in a
place where a White supremacist can push a button and erase all of that.
Right? Like, this was always going to be the outcome when technology and
the state mediate memory. And I think the same for law. For me as an
attorney, it is very, very interesting.

Right, like, not in a, 'I’m sitting back delighted way,' but it’s very
interesting to see how the law can be just dismantled, how it can become
easily speculative, how it can become fiction depending on who is in
power and who’s making the judgment. We see the Supreme Court
dismantling things left and right that we as a civil society have said,
'hey, these are the things that we need to be civil and for us to thrive
as a country,' right. Like they’re just, with a check mark, tearing it
all apart. So, for me, it always has been that law is just as much as
speculative practice as writing fiction. It is about structuring
reality. Law codifies what’s possible. Speculative practice imagines
what could be possible, right? Like, it is two sides of the same coin.
And law has always been the imagination of White supremacy. It is what
they have made real in terms of the boundaries of our society.

And so, in legal advocacy, I’m able to see the state’s rigid
timelines. You know, as a lawyer, whether it’s statute of limitations
for the people I was representing, eviction filings, criminal records.
These are all kinds of attempts to fix Black people in place, right, and
to set the bounds of how they can move and where they can move. What
folks can learn is speculative work, imagination work, helps us to open
and disrupt timelines, whether it’s community archives, whether it’s
oral futures, whether it’s non-linear maps of memory. These are the
kinds of tools that I use, but you know, there’s lots of different
tools that people use. And I think what’s important is that in my work,
whether it’s at PolicyLink, or in my work as an Afrofuturist, or my
previous work as an attorney, I don’t treat imagination as a luxury. It
is a survival technology. It is a technology that lives alongside of any
other, you know, whether it’s money, whether it’s whatever it may be.

It’s a tool for and technology for survival. And my work has been about
researching, looking at all the ways, different ways that people have
used imagination and speculation to be able to navigate the most
impossible conditions. So, like, for example, in my book, I talk about
Harriet Tubman and how she used nature and speculation, and voices, and
different things to navigate impossible conditions. And I think
practicing activism, as Ruth [Wilson Gilmore] tells us about
abolition, right? We can’t be just resisting harms. We have to be about
creating the new, creating the new worlds. And again, new might not be
brand spanking new [that] we just flipped it out of air, right, but it
could mean uprooting the old and repackaging it for modern times. I’m
not a person who tries to romanticize the past and say, 'oh, everything
in the past is great and would work in the present and we just need to
go back to that.'

But no, I think we do need to reshape things to be applicable to the
times that we’re in and some of the things about our times that we
can’t control. And I encourage the communities that I work with to
think of imagination, to think of speculation and storytelling,
narrative work as forms of policymaking. They are just as important as
the legal work that I do. We have a project at PolicyLink that is
strictly about narrative and about building out housing narrative. Like,
that is just as important a strategy as me getting a policy where people
can be represented if they’re being kicked out of their homes. Without
a narrative strategy, I would not have been able to advance those
policies, right? So, people being able to storytell, that’s what
convinces people. That’s what moves the needle every time. So, I see
these tools as just as important as, you know, your everyday other kinds
of political tools.

Particularly for this set of conversations in Volume 43, we’re talking
to different people at different stages of their career. Like, we have
some people very early on who are still in graduate school at some
level, and then people sort of like yourself, kinda outside but adjacent
to the academy. And I’m curious for you, if there was something you
would advocate for in terms of institutional spaces, what they could do?
In particular, schools and educational institutions. Because I think at
some level, almost everything you talk about has a really direct
implication for schools, for how people are educated, how people learn
about institutions and things like that. What are the things that you
would love to see in the context of the documentary record that we
associate with, like, education in school? What are the techniques? What
are the resources? What kind of new toolkits should we be advocating
for? Because ironically, one of the things that is still hyper-local is
schooling.

It’s under assault, no question, but it’s still essentially
hyper-local. So, you could see in a sort of fragmented dystopian future,
some school systems doing some things that are very much good and some
school systems, sadly, doing some things very much bad. So, what would
be something that you would advocate for in that space?

Yeah, I think I would advocate for more embedded community-based work. I
think often what happens in the university is about the community, but
not in the community. Like, it’s abstracted, right? You study the
community, but you don’t necessarily come in contact with them. And I
think there are models of things that we’ve seen where people are
embedded, like, whether it’s an externship or internship or whatever,
or a project that you have to do. There’s a project in Philadelphia
called the 'Dox Thrash House,' which was about restoring the home of
this artist named Dox Thrash from the 1930's, around the [Harlem]
Renaissance, but maybe a little bit after Harlem Renaissance time. And
part of the project was revived by a bunch of architectural students at
the University of Penn who then continued the project after they
graduated and have now, like, just made... [were] able to get a lot of
money to restore the home, all these different things, umm, educate the
community about this artist, set up community programs.

And again, started with a couple of students at the university who had
the foresight to, like, take this project, build it out over a couple of
years' time, and not just be limited to the university because even
after they graduated were still involved in it. So I think things like
that. I think from my own personal experience, going through law school,
none of that stuff I learned mattered until I was able to apply it,
until I was able to actually work in the community and apply the
knowledge. Otherwise, it was just super abstract cases about people’s
lives that obviously shift people’s lives, but you don’t know how
practically and how you can intervene in that until you’re actually in
the community doing things. And so, I was really dissatisfied with my
legal education until I had the chance to do an internship at a family
court and actually work with people and actually apply it.

So, I think that’s what I would consider. I have seen when folks
actually go into the community, and it doesn’t have to be \... like,
even if it’s not, like, your university may not host that, but you as a
person can have the opportunity to start within your own community. Go
to a community event, go to monthly community meetings, talk to people,
right. Like, you can start within your own community with your own self.
It doesn’t take necessarily a whole body of people or a university
backing to be able to do that work. It can be as simple as going to a
local community organization and [saying], 'hey, do you need support
archiving the history of your organization?' Start right there. Do a
workshop with community members at the local library about archiving
their family documents, right. So, there’s ways that we can intervene
and be in community, and support, and build up these kinds of practices
without relying on a university.

But if we are going to rely on it, I think those are some of the keys is
making sure you’re in contact, and your work is not abstracted, and
you’re not just studying people. Because then by the time you get out
there, right, like, it’s so disconnected and you have to catch up in
terms of, like, actually knowing real people and being able to apply it
in real time. And it may not fit one for one, but you can start now.

I appreciate that. And I think that’s actually a good place, good place
to stop. I really appreciate you taking the time to talk with me for
Scholarly Editing. I believe that our readership will get a lot from
your perspectives and your voice. And so, I truly want to thank you for
sharing that with us.
Of course. Anytime. Yeah. Thanks so much for thinking of me.
